common_name: Henry Duelberg Salvia (Vigorous Native Texas Blue Mealy Sage Selection)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; Henry Duelberg's exceptional drought tolerance once established makes it ideal for low-irrigation landscapes — water weekly for the first season, then only during extended drought.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun; Henry Duelberg is a superior selection of mealy blue sage (Salvia farinacea) — a Texas native wildflower — chosen for its outstanding garden performance: larger, more vigorous plant habit, more robust flower spike production, and excellent persistence compared to typical nursery-grown mealy sage seedlings. Named for Henry Duelberg, a San Antonio area rancher and plant enthusiast who grew this selection for years, the cultivar was popularized by plantsman Scott Ogden and represents one of the most valuable native perennial salvias for Gulf South gardens. The lavender-blue to rich violet-blue flower spikes — each 6–12 inches tall with densely packed whorls of small tubular flowers along white-farinose (mealy-powdery) stems — bloom from early spring through heavy frost, providing an extremely long ornamental season. The extended bloom, exceptional heat and drought tolerance, deer resistance, and powerful attraction to hummingbirds, monarchs, swallowtails, and native bees make Henry Duelberg Salvia one of the top performers for zone 8-10 pollinator gardens and xeriscape designs. In zone 9, established plants are essentially self-sustaining on rainfall alone after the first season. The fine-textured blue flower spikes combine well with coarser-textured native companions — Salvia greggii, Lantana, Gulf muhly — in low-maintenance native plant gardens.

common_name: Blue Fortune Agastache (Long-Blooming Lavender-Blue Anise Hyssop Hybrid)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Blue Fortune requires excellent drainage — it is one of the few commonly grown ornamental perennials that truly suffers in heavy clay soil or standing water, making raised beds and amended beds essential in clay-heavy Gulf South soils.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to average soil in full sun; Blue Fortune is an interspecific hybrid agastache (Agastache foeniculum x A. rugosa) combining the anise fragrance and lavender-blue color of anise hyssop with improved heat and humidity tolerance from the Korean mint parent. The long, cylindrical flower spikes in soft lavender-blue to violet-blue bloom from summer through fall, creating an extended pollinator resource that attracts hummingbirds, monarch butterflies, swallowtails, bumble bees, and numerous native bee species in large numbers. The anise-mint fragrance of crushed leaves is pleasant and fresh — the foliage is edible and can be used as an anise-flavored herb for teas, desserts, and salads. Blue Fortune is more persistent in zone 8-10 than straight Agastache foeniculum, making it one of the more reliable agastache options for Gulf South gardens where high summer humidity and heat can challenge the genus. The upright, stiffly branching habit and lavender-blue flower color make Blue Fortune an outstanding companion for warm-orange and yellow fall-blooming perennials — Rudbeckia, Helenium, Salvia greggii — where the cool blue provides complementary contrast to warm fall colors. Excellent drainage is the most critical cultural requirement — plants in poorly drained clay soils are short-lived regardless of other conditions.

common_name: Inca Red Drop Pepper (Fruity Andean Hot Pepper with Small Elongated Red Fruits)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 10–12 weeks before transplanting; transplant into fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Capsicum baccatum species benefit from a heat mat during germination — the higher soil temperature (80°F+) significantly improves germination speed and percentage compared to ambient room temperature germination.

directions: Set out transplants in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun after the last frost date; Inca Red Drop is a Capsicum baccatum pepper from the Andean tradition of Peru — the species group that also includes Aji Amarillo and Aji Cristal, characterized by the distinctly fruity, citrusy, tropical flavor profile that distinguishes Andean peppers from the more neutral heat of C. annuum varieties. Inca Red Drop produces small to medium (2–3 inch), elongated, pendant, bright-red fruits with a pointed tip — the 'drop' shape giving the variety its name. The heat level is medium to medium-hot (30,000–60,000 SHU) — more heat than a jalapeño but less than a habanero — with the fruity, berry-like undertones characteristic of quality C. baccatum selections. The fruit flavor combines the fruity complexity of the aji family with a clean, bright heat that builds persistently. In Peruvian and Bolivian cooking, aji baccatum varieties are used fresh, dried, and as the base for sauces and marinades where the fruity complexity is as important as the heat level. The long season to maturity (90–120 days from transplant) is well-suited to zone 9's extended warm season. The ornamental value of the small red pendant fruits on attractive plants makes Inca Red Drop equally at home in ornamental pepper displays and in dedicated vegetable gardens.

common_name: Brilliant Red Tropical Hibiscus (Classic Large Single Red Hibiscus rosa-sinensis)

planting.note: Plant container plants after the last frost in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Brilliant is one of the most continuously free-blooming tropical hibiscus varieties in Gulf South summer heat — even in August when other hibiscus varieties have reduced bloom, Brilliant typically maintains good flower production.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Brilliant is one of the classic large-single tropical hibiscus cultivars — a time-tested variety producing very large (5–6 inch), brilliant solid-red, single flowers with the typical flared, five-petaled hibiscus form and prominent staminal column. The pure, intense red of 'Brilliant' — without the pink undertones of some red hibiscus or the orange overtones of others — is the quintessential tropical hibiscus red that defines the color archetype. Individual flowers last one day but are produced in continuous succession throughout the warm season on well-fertilized, adequately watered plants — a mature hibiscus in peak condition can produce 20–40 open flowers daily. Hibiscus rosa-sinensis is the national flower of Malaysia and a widely revered cultural symbol across Southeast Asia and the Pacific — Brilliant's classic single-red form is the type most closely associated with this symbolic status. In zone 9, Brilliant grows as a large perennial shrub in protected locations but may be severely damaged or killed by hard freezes — container-grown specimens provide the flexibility to overwinter indoors during cold spells. The continuous bloom, vivid color, and hummingbird attraction make Brilliant one of the most effective tropical accent plants for Gulf South summer container displays and protected courtyard gardens.

common_name: Patio Snacker Cucumber (Compact Container-Friendly Snacking Cucumber)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Patio Snacker is specifically bred for container and small-space growing — the compact vines require minimal support and grow well in large containers (5-gallon minimum) where standard vining cucumbers would be impractical.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Patio Snacker is a compact, semi-vining cucumber variety selected for container and patio growing — producing shorter, more manageable vines (18–30 inches) than standard cucumber varieties while maintaining prolific fruit production of small (3–4 inch) snacking-size cucumbers. The small fruit size and compact plant habit are ideally matched for patio container growing: the cucumbers are sized for fresh snacking without slicing, and the plant fits in a large container (5-gallon minimum, 10-gallon ideal) with a simple stake or small trellis. Patio Snacker produces crisp, thin-skinned, nearly seedless cucumbers with mild, sweet flavor and no bitterness — characteristic of the modern small-cucumber/snacking types bred for fresh eating without the preparation steps (peeling, seed removal) required for large slicing varieties. In zone 9, cucumbers are most productive in the spring shoulder season (February–April) and the fall shoulder season (August–September) — summer heat reduces fruiting and increases disease pressure. The compact size and container suitability make Patio Snacker particularly useful for zone 9 gardeners who can move containers to optimize microclimate conditions during the challenging summer transition period.
